reason: 2025 NATO Hague Summit set a 3.5%-of-GDP core defence target. Raising the lane to the commitment; this is a policy choice within (now at the top of) the 2.0-3.5% band.
decision_authority: ordinary-legislative (within band); supermajority if it breaches the balance rule
effect_on_borrowed_share: increases the gap by 146,149 $M unless offset by revenue or another lane cut
beneficiary_impact: none direct; raises the revenue or cut required elsewhere

reason: US government/compulsory health is ~2x the OECD cost share (14.3% vs 7.1% of GDP) for narrower coverage. A 20% cost-per-outcome reduction (delivery/pricing reform, not coverage cuts) lowers the lane.
effect_on_borrowed_share: reduces the gap by 395,046 $M (22% of it)
beneficiary_impact: coverage protected; lower price-per-service to providers

new_rate.target: close the gap on the base, not the rate

reason: FY2025 OASDI cost (1,580,673) exceeds dedicated receipts (1,283,736) by 296,937 $M, covered by trust-fund drawdown. The US uniquely caps the taxed wage base; peers (Germany 18.6%, Japan 18.3%) tax at higher rates often without a cap. Lift the cap rather than the rate.
effect_on_borrowed_share: closes the Social Security trust-fund gap; does not touch the general-fund deficit
beneficiary_impact: benefits protected; higher earners pay on more of their wages
